desc: LA's largest native bee. Males golden "teddy bear bee"; females jet black. Wood excavator.

fm.Size: ⅔ in–1 in; among largest NA bees
fm.Nesting: Tunnels in dead wood; sawdust partitions

desc: Both sexes dark metallic blue-black. Nests in dead yucca/agave.
Malvaceae specialist. Dense aggregation nester in sandy soil.
Builds distinctive mud chimney entrance turrets (diagnostic).
Large, fast-flying desert digger. Important native desert pollinator.

fm.Nesting: Deep ground burrows in desert sand

desc: Brilliant metallic green. Ground-nesting in communal aggregations.

fm.Hair: Metallic green head/thorax; striped abdomen (♀) or green (♂)

desc: Most common native bee. Weakly eusocial — queens found nests, produce workers.

fm.Hair: Dark; faint metallic sheen; thin pale bands

desc: Small metallic, among most abundant CA bees. Social with queen/worker castes.
Cuts circular leaf pieces to line nest cells. Ventral abdominal scopa unique to family.

fm.Hair: Dark; pale bands; orange ventral scopa when loaded
fm.Nesting: Cavity nester; bee hotels; leaf-lined cells

desc: Excellent early-season pollinator. Nests in cavities, partitioned with mud.

fm.Nesting: Cavity nester; mud cell partitions; bee hotels

desc: Introduced. Males territorial, rams other bees in flight. Females card plant fibers for nests.

fm.Size: ♂: ½ in–⅔ in. ♀: ⅓ in–½ in.
fm.Hair: Black with bold yellow lateral spots
fm.Nesting: Cavity nester; cells lined with carded plant hair

desc: Solitary ground-nester on Prunus blooms. Turret-entrance burrows.
Conspicuous spring bee visiting willows and early Asteraceae.

fm.Hair: Dark; dense tawny thorax; pale scopae

desc: Large, robust leafcutter. Conspicuous buzzy flight. Cuts large leaf pieces.

fm.Hair: Dark; dense grayish-white thorax; pale bands

desc: Specialist on Cucurbita (squash/pumpkin). Active at dawn before honeybees. Ground nester near squash plants.
A large, conspicuous spring mining bee visiting cherry and other Rosaceae blooms.

fm.Size: ½ in–⅔ in; large for Andrena
fm.Hair: Dark; dense golden thorax; pale hind leg scopae
fm.Nesting: Ground; solitary; often in paths and banks

desc: A resin-collecting bee specialized on Larrea (creosote bush). Lines nest cells with plant resins.

fm.Nesting: Ground; lines cells with Larrea resin (diagnostic behavior)

desc: A smaller carpenter bee than X. varipuncta. Dark with blue-green metallic sheen. Desert habitats.

fm.Hair: Dark with blue-green metallic sheen; minimal pale hair
fm.Nesting: Excavates wood; also nests in yucca/agave stalks

desc: Pomona sweat bee — tiny brilliant metallic green bee. One of the few eusocial (colony-forming) sweat bees. Ground-nesting. Multiple females share a nest. Important wildflower pollinator.
Perdita minima — among the smallest bees on Earth at under 2mm. Specialist on spurge (Euphorbia) flowers. Nearly invisible without magnification. Ground-nesting in sandy soil.

fm.Color: Dark; nearly smallest bee in world

desc: Specialist pollinator of Opuntia (prickly pear) cactus. Dense aggregation nester. Desert and desert-edge.
Medium mining bee common on Encelia and Asteraceae. Conspicuous in coastal sage scrub in spring.

fm.Hair: Dark; pale thoracic pile; pale scopae

desc: Robust digger bee that collects floral oils from Krameria. Rapid buzzy flight.

fm.Pollen: Scopae; also collects oils from Krameria

desc: Larger than most halictids. Pale pruinose (powdery) appearance. Common on Asteraceae.
Fast-flying bee that nests in vertical clay banks, extending entrance with mud chimney. Buzz pollinates.

fm.Nesting: Clay banks; builds chimney extension at entrance; often in dense aggregations on road cuts

desc: Large, fast-flying bee common on sunflowers and Asteraceae. Dense pale thoracic hair.

fm.Hair: Dense pale cream thorax; banded abdomen

desc: Metallic blue mason bee associated with Ribes and blueberries. Nests in cavities.

fm.Nesting: Cavity nester; bee hotels; pre-existing holes

desc: Small gray bee that cuts circular leaf pieces to line nest cells. Important alfalfa pollinator.
Uses plant resins instead of leaf pieces to build nest. Cavity nester in wood and stems.

fm.Nesting: Cavities — uses plant resins not leaves

desc: Males have extremely long antennae. Fast-flying. Ground-nesting. Important sunflower pollinator.
Small brilliant green bee nesting in rotting wood — unusual for a sweat bee. Solitary.

fm.Nesting: Rotting wood — unusual for a sweat bee

desc: Robust furry bee nesting in sandy ground. Important spring pollinator of native wildflowers.
Japanese mason bee introduced for orchard pollination. Metallic blue-green. Extremely efficient fruit pollinator.
Black bee with white hair bands. Cuts neat circular leaf pieces. Important native pollinator.
Ground-nesting bee building chimney-like turrets over nest entrance. Specialist of Sphaeralcea (mallow).
One of the most common native bees in LA County. Tiny metallic green. Ground-nesting. Visits many wildflowers.

fm.Nesting: Ground — dense aggregations in compacted soil
fm.Season: Feb–Nov; nearly year-round in mild areas

desc: Small dark sweat bee. Semi-social — multiple females share nest entrance. Very common garden visitor.
Tiny dark metallic sweat bee. Enormous genus — hundreds of species, many undescribed. Abundant.
Small dark bee with punctate metasoma. Very common LA County sweat bee on buckwheat and sunflowers.
Specialist bee of Asteraceae. Nests in aggregations in sandy soil. Important pollinator.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with pale hair bands

desc: Small green-tinged mining bee. Spring flier on willows and early wildflowers.
Large Asian resin bee now established in CA. Nests in wood cavities. Displaces native cavity nesters.
Metallic blue-green mason bee. Important native pollinator of fruit trees and wildflowers. Cavity nester.

fm.Nesting: Cavities — existing holes in wood, stems

desc: Japanese mason bee accidentally introduced. Metallic blue. Nests in existing holes. Expanding range.
Most important managed pollinator. European introduction. Feral colonies common in LA. Social, perennial.

fm.Nesting: Cavities — feral colonies in trees, buildings

desc: Cleptoparasitic bee with pointed abdomen. Enters leafcutter nests to lay eggs. Larvae eat host provisions.

fm.Color: Black with white hair bands; pointed abdomen

desc: Bright metallic green head/thorax, banded yellow-black abdomen. Ground-nesting. Common garden visitor.
Tiny black and yellow-faced bee. Carries pollen internally in crop. Nests in hollow stems and beetle borings.

fm.Color: Black with yellow face marks; wasp-like

desc: Medium brown furry bee that lines nest cells with cellophane-like secretion. On Asteraceae.

fm.Description: Medium brown furry bee that lines nest cells with cellophane-like secretion

desc: Robust furry bee nesting in clay banks. Fast flight with loud buzz. Important native pollinator.

fm.Description: Robust furry bee nesting in clay banks

desc: Large metallic blue-black carpenter bee. Desert subspecies. Nests in dead agave stalks and soft wood.
Rare nocturnal bee active at dusk. Enlarged ocelli for low-light vision. Visits evening primrose.

fm.Color: Dark with enlarged ocelli for low light

desc: Small dark bee that bores into dead wood for nesting. Specialist on cactus flowers (Opuntia, Cylindropuntia).
Medium-sized golden-brown sweat bee. Variable sociality — social in warm areas, solitary in cool. Widespread.

fm.Nesting: Ground — social in warm areas, solitary in cool

desc: Small dark carpenter bee nesting in flower stalks and soft pithy stems. Less destructive than large Xylocopa.

fm.Description: Small dark carpenter bee nesting in flower stalks and soft pithy stems

desc: Red and yellow cleptoparasitic bee. Enters mining bee nests to lay eggs. Larvae eat host provisions.
Red-abdomen cleptoparasite of sweat bees. Enters Lasioglossum nests. Named for blood-red color.
Ground-nesting bee lining cells with waterproof cellophane-like secretion. On buckwheat flowers.
Males with extremely long antennae. Fast-flying. Nests in ground. Important pollinator of many wildflowers.

fm.Color: Brown-gray; males with very long antennae

desc: Tiny blue-green carpenter bee nesting in pithy stems. Mothers guard larvae and provision progressively.
Medium fuzzy mining bee active in spring. Important early-season pollinator of native wildflowers.
Black and white cleptoparasitic bee. Parasitizes long-horned bees. Bold spotted pattern.
Fuzzy mining bee with dense scopal hairs. Spring-flying on willows and native shrubs.

fm.Color: Black with dense pale scopal hairs

desc: Female entirely metallic green; male has green head and yellow-banded abdomen. Ground nester.

fm.Color: ♀ all green; ♂ green head + yellow abdomen

desc: Robust pale desert bee. Males patrol nesting aggregations competing for emerging females. Palo verde specialist.
Robust black and yellow bee mimicking bumblebees. Fast erratic flight. Nests in cliffs and adobe walls.

fm.Color: Black with yellow thoracic hair — bumblebee mimic

desc: Bright metallic green bee with pale leg hairs. Ground-nesting. Visits many flower types.
Actually a pollen wasp, not a bee — convergently evolved pollen-collecting from Phacelia flowers.

fm.Behavior: Pollen wasp — convergent evolution with bees

desc: Medium furry mining bee with prominent facial hair. Ground-nesting in sandy soil. Spring wildflower pollinator.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with pale thoracic hair

desc: Small mining bee nesting in cracks of sidewalks and compacted soil. One of the first bees active in spring.
Mining bee specializing on Malvaceae (mallow family) flowers. Pollen collected from Sphaeralcea, Malva.
Dark metallic blue mason bee. Nests in beetle borings and hollow stems. Important spring pollinator.
Mountain mason bee of mid-elevation meadows. Metallic blue. Important wildflower pollinator.
Large dark leafcutter bee. Uses plant resins mixed with stone chips to build nest cells. Impressive mandibles.

fm.Nesting: Cavities — uses resin and stone chips

desc: Rocky-substrate nesting digger bee. Builds individual cells in sandstone and cliff faces. Desert species.
Medium sweat bee with distinctive orange-tinted legs. Semi-social — shared nests. Common garden visitor.
Small metallic sweat bee. Guard bees block nest entrance with head. Social behavior varies with latitude.
Vivid metallic gold-green bee. Ground-nesting. Visits wide variety of flowers.
Tiny yellow and black bee — one of the smallest bees in the world at <⅛ in. Ground-nesting specialist.
Fast-flying bee with extremely long antennae (males). Specialist of Asteraceae especially sunflowers.
Tiny black bee with white face markings. Wasp-like. Carries pollen internally in crop. Nests in hollow stems.

fm.Color: Black with white face marks; wasp-like

desc: Dark bee with two pale spots on abdomen. Males with very long antennae. Important late-season pollinator.

fm.Color: Dark with two pale abdominal spots; ♂ long antennae

desc: Medium fuzzy mining bee with greenish-tinted abdomen. Important pollinator of spring wildflowers and fruit trees.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with pale thoracic hair

desc: Ground-nesting specialist of Opuntia cactus flowers. Chimney-turret nest entrance. Desert and desert-edge.
Early spring bee specializing on willow catkins. Important early-season pollinator when few other bees active.
Native wool carder bee — scrapes plant hairs for nest material. Less aggressive than European A. manicatum.
Medium bee with iridescent green abdominal bands. Important alfalfa pollinator. Dense nesting aggregations.

fm.Nesting: Ground — huge aggregations in alkaline soil

desc: Small dark sweat bee. Very common but easily overlooked. Important pollinator of native wildflowers.
Dark-legged digger bee of desert-edge habitats. Oil-collecting from Krameria. Ground nesting.
Black mining bee with distinctive gray thoracic hair. Solitary ground-nester. Spring specialist — among the first bees active each year. Visits willows, fruit trees.
Unusual bee that collects floral oils instead of nectar. Specialist on loosestrife (Lysimachia) flowers. Waterproofs nest cells with oil.

fm.Behavior: Collects floral oils — waterproofs brood cells

desc: Large bee specializing on squash/pumpkin flowers. Active at dawn — often found sleeping inside closed squash blossoms by midday. Critical crop pollinator.

fm.Habitat: Squash and pumpkin flowers — dawn specialist
fm.Behavior: Sleeps inside closed squash blossoms by midday

desc: Small wasp-like bee (virtually hairless, no pollen baskets). Cleptoparasite — lays eggs in other bees' nests; larva kills host egg and eats provisions. Social parasite.

fm.Color: Red-orange and black; wasp-like; no body hair
fm.Behavior: Cleptoparasite — enters other bee nests to lay eggs

desc: Black-and-white felt-patterned cuckoo bee. Parasitizes Colletes bees. Thick white hair patches form distinctive pattern. No pollen-collecting apparatus.
Desert calliopsis — tiny specialist bee of desert wildflowers. Ground-nesting in sandy soil. Part of diverse desert bee fauna.
Tiny bee (3-5mm) specializing on evening primrose (Oenothera). One of the smallest solitary bees. Ground-nesting. Active primarily at dawn.
Large fuzzy golden bee resembling a teddy bear. Strong fast flier. Nests in soft sandstone and mud banks. Recently documented in SoCal.

Valley carpenter bee — largest native bee in California. ♀ black; ♂ golden-tan with green eyes. Bores nest tunnels in dead wood and structural timber. Loud buzzing hover. Important native pollinator of passionflower and many garden plants.

fm.Habitat: Dead wood, fences, eaves — bores nest tunnels

desc: Leafcutter bee with orange-red ventral scopa. Visits sunflowers and other composites. Common in late summer gardens.
Metallic green bee. ♀ green head+thorax with striped abdomen. ♂ entirely green. Generalist pollinator. Ground-nesting.
Black mining bee with dense black hair. Spring specialist on willow catkins. One of the first bees flying each year.

fm.Color: Black with dense black body hair

desc: Mining bee with distinctive white facial hair. Spring generalist. Ground-nesting in bare soil patches.
Not actually a bee — parasitoid wasp that attacks solitary bee nests. Included here because it's most commonly seen at bee nesting sites. Thread-waisted.
Medium bee secreting cellophane-like lining for nest cells. Specialist on composites (sunflower family). Fall-flying.
Tiny yellow-and-black bee specializing on desert flowers. Ground-nesting in sand. One of the smallest bees in the world.
Medium bee — ♂ with extremely long antennae that wrap around during sleep. Nests in ground. Specialist on legume flowers.
Horse-fly carpenter bee — metallic blue-black carpenter bee. Smaller than Valley carpenter bee. Nests in dead wood and plant stems. Fast powerful flier. Important pollinator.
Fine striped sweat bee — brilliant metallic green head/thorax, ♀ with striped abdomen. Very common at flowers. Ground-nesting. Important urban pollinator.

fm.Color: ♀ green head/thorax + striped abdomen; ♂ all green

desc: Arizona small carpenter bee — tiny metallic blue-green bee nesting in pithy stems. Breaks open dead stems to create nest cells. Semi-social — mother guards larvae.
Bindweed turret bee — medium fuzzy bee building tall chimney turrets over soil nest entrance. Specialist pollinator of morning glory and bindweed (Convolvulus). Common in weedy urban lots.

fm.Habitat: Bindweed/morning glory — chimney turrets on soil

desc: California digger-cuckoo bee — large black-and-white bee. Cleptoparasite of Anthophora digger bees. Enters host nests to lay eggs. Hovers near nesting banks scouting hosts.

fm.Behavior: Cleptoparasite — scouts digger bee nesting banks

desc: Sumac mining bee — tiny yellow-and-black specialist bee on sumac (Rhus) and lemonade berry. One of the many Perdita species endemic to California. Ground-nesting in sandy soil.
Squash bee — large robust bee specializing on squash/pumpkin flowers. Active at dawn — enters squash flowers early. Males sleep inside closed blossoms. Ground-nesting.
Sweet nomad bee — small wasp-like cuckoo bee. Cleptoparasite of Andrena mining bees — enters host nests to lay eggs. Red-orange and black; virtually hairless.
Short digger bee — robust fast-flying bee nesting in clay banks. Important native pollinator. Hovers like a hummingbird. Active in spring on salvia and other flowers.
Faithful leafcutter bee — medium leafcutter bee cutting circular pieces from leaves to line nest cells. Carries pollen on ventral scopa. Common in late summer gardens.

fm.Color: Dark; pale abdominal bands; orange belly scopa

desc: Oval-headed sweat bee — tiny dark sweat bee with oval head shape. Ground-nesting. Generalist pollinator. Very common but easily overlooked.
Colorado mason bee — small metallic blue-green mason bee. Nests in pre-existing holes. Important spring pollinator of native wildflowers in foothill habitats.
California poppy fairy bee — tiny specialist bee on California poppies. Among the smallest bees — under 5mm. Ground-nesting in sandy soil near poppy fields. Spring flight.
Northern rotund-resin bee — small round bee using plant resin to construct nests. Black with yellow markings. Nests in pre-existing cavities. Visits composite flowers.

fm.Color: Black with yellow spots; round body

desc: Spotted woolcarder — medium bee with bold yellow spots on dark body. Males aggressively defend flower territories by body-slamming intruders. Females card plant fibers for nests.
Globe mallow bee — medium fuzzy bee specializing on globe mallow (Sphaeralcea). Orange mallow pollen loads diagnostic. Desert-urban interface.

fm.Color: Fuzzy gray-brown; orange mallow pollen loads

desc: Bumblebee-like digger bee — large robust bee that closely resembles a bumblebee. Nests in clay banks. Fast powerful flier. Visits many flowers. Important native pollinator.

fm.Color: Fuzzy black and yellow — bumblebee mimic

desc: Saddest digger bee — large robust bee nesting in soil banks. Despite the name (tristissima = 'saddest'), it's an active vigorous pollinator of spring wildflowers.
Verbesina longhorn-cuckoo bee — cleptoparasitic bee. Lays eggs in nests of longhorn bees (Melissodes). Spiny abdomen. Distinctively patterned black-and-white.

fm.Color: Black with white hair patches; spiny abdomen

desc: Rose-loving calliopsis — small mining bee visiting evening primrose and other flowers. Ground-nesting. Males patrol flower patches. Desert-urban interface.
Illustrated woolcarder bee — large colorful bee with bold yellow markings. Collects plant fibers ('wool') for nests. Males territorial on flower patches.
Collected woolcarder — medium woolcarder bee with yellow spots. On various flowers. Nests with plant fiber. Part of the diverse California Anthidium fauna.
Blue-phacelia miner — mining bee visiting phacelia and other wildflowers. Spring specialist. Ground-nesting in bare soil. Important native wildflower pollinator.
Punctate masked bee — tiny wasp-like bee with yellow face marks and punctured integument. Nests in beetle holes and hollow stems. Carries pollen internally.

fm.Color: Black with yellow face; virtually hairless

desc: Red-tailed micro shortface — tiny halictid bee. Among the smallest bees in LA County. Ground-nesting. Specialist on phacelia flowers. Red-tipped abdomen.
Bearded calliopsis — small mining bee with distinctive facial hair. Ground-nesting in sandy soil. Visits evening primrose and other wildflowers.
Clarkia evening bee — small bee specializing on Clarkia flowers. Part of the small family Melittidae. Ground-nesting. Active in late afternoon. California native specialist.
Baker's phacelia shortface — tiny halictid specializing on phacelia. Among the smallest bees. Ground-nesting in sandy soil. Named for entomologist C.F. Baker.
Grinnell's small mason bee — small mason bee nesting in pre-existing holes in wood and stems. Named for zoologist Joseph Grinnell. On native wildflowers.
Frugal leafcutter bee — medium leafcutter cutting precise circles from leaves. Carries pollen on ventral scopa. Visits various flowers. Common in gardens.

fm.Color: Dark; pale bands; orange ventral scopa

desc: Yellow-collared masked bee — tiny wasp-like bee with yellow collar and face marks. Nests in hollow stems. Carries pollen internally in crop. Visits many flowers.

fm.Color: Black with yellow collar and face marks

desc: California chelostoma — small dark mason bee nesting in beetle holes. Specialist on Phacelia flowers. Ground-nesting in pre-existing cavities. California native.
Lonely mining bee — solitary ground-nesting bee of spring wildflower habitats. Visits various flowers. Part of the enormous Andrena genus (~80 species in California).
Common longhorn bee — medium bee with long ♂ antennae. Visits sunflower family flowers in late summer. Ground-nesting. Sleeps on flower heads.
Slevin's cellophane bee — solitary bee secreting cellophane-like polyester lining for nest cells. Ground-nesting. Waterproof and antimicrobial nest coating. On native flowers.
Parallel leafcutter bee — large leafcutter with orange ventral scopa. Cuts circular pieces from leaves. One of the largest North American leafcutters.
Shining small-mason bee — small metallic green-blue mason bee. Nests in beetle holes and hollow stems. Important native pollinator of wildflowers.
Pomona longhorn bee — medium bee named for Pomona, CA. Squash specialist. Males sleep inside closed squash flowers. Ground-nesting. Dawn flier.
White-fronted small-mason — tiny mason bee with white face. Nests in pre-existing holes in wood or stems. Spring wildflower pollinator.
Oblique longhorn bee — medium bee with long ♂ antennae on late-summer composites. Important pollinator of native sunflowers.
Los Angeles leafcutter bee — leafcutter bee named for Los Angeles. Cuts circular leaf pieces to line nest cells. On native flowers. California species.
Red-flower slender mason — tiny mason bee on red-flowering plants. Nests in beetle holes. Important native pollinator of foothill wildflowers.
Unusual-bodied mining bee — small ground-nesting bee with distinctive body shape. On native wildflowers. Part of California's diverse native bee fauna.
Nimble squash bee — medium bee specializing on squash and pumpkin flowers. Active at dawn before other bees. Ground-nesting near squash plantings.
Golden-haired mining bee — medium bee with golden thoracic hair. Spring-flying. Ground-nesting. Important pollinator of early wildflowers.
Black-legged mining bee — medium dark bee with black legs. Ground-nesting. On native flowers. Spring flier.
Eight-toothed cuckoo leafcutter — cleptoparasitic bee. Enters leafcutter bee nests to lay eggs. Pointed abdomen for breaking into sealed cells. No pollen-collecting apparatus.
Edwards' digger bee — large robust fast-flying bee. Nests in clay banks. Important native pollinator visiting many flower types. Hovers like a hummingbird.
Crotch's digger bee — medium robust bee nesting in soil. Named for pioneer West Coast entomologist G.R. Crotch. On various flowers. Spring flier.
Desdemona fairy bee — extremely tiny specialist bee. Among the smallest bees in the world (~3mm). Ground-nesting in sandy soil. On specific wildflowers.

fm.Color: Yellow and black; one of world's smallest bees

desc: Apical leafcutter — medium leafcutter bee cutting circular pieces from rose and other leaves. Introduced from Europe. Nests in pre-existing holes.
Three-signed fairy bee — tiny (~4mm) specialist bee on lotus and related plants. Ground-nesting. Among the world's smallest bees.
Fiddleneck longhorn bee — medium bee with long ♂ antennae specializing on fiddleneck (Amsinckia) flowers. Spring flier.
California resin-leafcutter — medium bee combining resin and leaf pieces for nest construction. Unusual double-material nesting. California native.

fm.Color: Dark; uses both resin AND leaf pieces for nest

desc: Spotted mini-digger cuckoo — small cleptoparasitic bee with spotted body. Targets ground-nesting bees.
Crescent longhorn bee — medium bee with ♂ long antennae. On various wildflowers. Spring-flying. Ground-nesting.
Formula nomad bee — small cleptoparasitic bee. Red-and-black wasp mimic. Enters Andrena nests to lay eggs.
Creosote digger-cuckoo bee — cleptoparasitic bee targeting Anthophora nests near creosote bush. Black with white hair patches.
Crotch's nomad bee — small wasp-like cuckoo bee. Red-and-yellow. Parasitizes mining bee (Andrena) nests. Named for entomologist G.R. Crotch.
Creosote small-mason — tiny mason bee associated with creosote bush habitat. Nests in beetle holes. Desert specialist.
Davidson's longhorn bee — medium bee with very long ♂ antennae. Named for early CA collector Davidson. On native flowers.
Ehrhorn's rotund-resin bee — tiny round bee using plant resin for nest construction. On native flowers. Named for entomologist Ehrhorn.

fm.Color: Black with yellow spots; round body

desc: Singular cuckoo bee — cleptoparasitic bee entering host nests (Diadasia turret bees) to lay eggs. Uncommon. On desert flowers scouting host nests.
Mesilla cuckoo bee — small cleptoparasitic bee targeting Colletes cellophane bee nests. Red-and-black wasp-like appearance.
Woodland mason bee — small dark mason bee of woodland habitats. Nests in dead wood. Important native pollinator.
Wide-striped dark bee — small cleptoparasitic bee targeting Megachile and Osmia nests. Dark with pale bands.

fm.Color: Dark with wide pale bands; parasitic

desc: Separated cuckoo bee — large black-and-white cleptoparasitic bee targeting Anthophora digger bee nests. Hovers near nesting banks.

fm.Color: Black with white hair patches; parasitic

desc: Conspicuous masked bee — tiny black bee with yellow face markings ('mask'). Nests in beetle holes. Carries pollen internally in crop. Unusual for bees.
Robust longhorn bee — medium bee with long ♂ antennae. On composites in late summer. Ground-nesting. Important late-season pollinator.
Olivaceous mining bee — medium olive-green tinged bee. Ground-nesting. On native wildflowers. Spring flier.
Glorious mason bee — small metallic blue-green mason bee. On native wildflowers. Nests in beetle holes.
Yellow-banded digger bee — robust fuzzy bee with yellow thoracic bands. Fast flier. Ground-nesting. Important native pollinator.
